items: The MHD simulations are non-relativistic and omit explicit cosmic-ray transport, radiative losses, and anisotropic conduction; the reconnection-driven re-acceleration claim therefore rests on indirect evidence and numerical (implicit) resistivity.

The jet injection and magnetic-arch configuration are presented largely as a single fiducial setup; parameter-space exploration (e.g., dependence on magnetic field strength/geometry, jet power/velocity, viewing angle) is limited.

Polarization and Faraday rotation measurements, which could directly probe magnetic field ordering and strength along the cold front, are not presented; such data would be a powerful corroboration of the magnetic-layer hypothesis.

The spectral index analysis relies on broadband imaging, but systematics (absolute flux scale, beam mismatch, deconvolution biases, spectral curvature) and uncertainties are not quantified in detail; spectral aging or curvature modeling (JP/KP/tribble) is not attempted.

The X-ray evidence for a plasma depletion layer is suggestive but not quantified (e.g., significance of the deviation from the best-fit model, temperature structure across the discontinuity to confirm cold-front nature vs. shock).
A few inconsistencies/typos and unit ambiguities (e.g., ρ0 units listed as cm^-3 rather than g cm^-3; “Kelven-Helmholtz”), and inconsistent counts of sub-bands used for spectral mapping.
The physical plausibility of the adopted magnetic field amplitude (B1 ~ 60 μG) at the cold front could be motivated more clearly with observational constraints or scaling arguments.

While the paper cites foundational cold-front and WAT literature, a more explicit comparison to polarization/RM studies of cold fronts and jet bending in clusters would contextualize the magnetic-layer strengths and coherence scales assumed.
A quantitative contrast with the ram-pressure bending scenario (e.g., momentum flux vs. ram pressure estimates) would strengthen the case that magnetic tension is the dominant bending agent here.
Polarisation: only one epoch, single frequency, and EVPA not absolutely calibrated; no Faraday rotation analysis; limits physical interpretation of magnetic field geometry.
Kinematics: J2’s separation decreases from 2017 to 2018 despite an overall positive trend; this raises questions about component identification and (u,v) coverage effects.
The jet–ISM density estimate relies on several strong and uncertain assumptions (e.g., head area from a large half-opening angle, strong-shock βh ≈ β/4), with no stated uncertainties.

The revised black hole mass (down to 4.4 × 10^7 M☉) depends on beaming-corrected scaling relations that are debated for blazars (line selection, continuum contamination) and is a large shift from earlier estimates.
Absence of multi-frequency VLBI prevents spectral index mapping, core shift assessment, RM determination, and depolarisation analysis.
No phase-referenced astrometry; the core assumed stationary despite potential blending and opacity-related shifts, which could bias proper motion estimates.
Limited number of epochs post-2010 flare; the J1 “stationarity” is only marginally significant (μ = -0.006 ± 0.004 mas/yr).

Some equation numbering/notation inconsistencies and typographical issues (e.g., duplicated equation labels, ≥ rendering), and ambiguous statements (e.g., two different jet power values in the same paragraph).
The polarisation discussion occasionally implies magnetic field ordering without EVPA calibration or RM constraints; should be more tentative.
Limited comparison to polarisation properties at high rest frequencies in blazars and to young/GPS/CSS populations at similar redshifts.
Could better situate opening angle assumptions using recent MOJAVE distributions and discuss recollimation shocks as an internal alternative to ISM-triggered standing shocks.
Spatial correlation analyses with receptor maps do not appear to control for spatial autocorrelation (e.g., spin tests/BrainSMASH), risking inflated significance due to regional smoothness.

The insomnia classification dataset is modest in size (n≈101) and demographically different (older) than the discovery cohort, raising overfitting and cohort-shift concerns; hyperparameter tuning and nested CV details are limited.
Static RSFC only; no exploration of dynamic functional connectivity which may capture arousal/attentional fluctuations more sensitively.

Limited reporting on feature standardization and hyperparameter optimization within folds for SVR/SVM; risk of minor information leakage or optimistic estimates if preprocessing was not strictly nested.

The “significant edges” that form the biomarker are not fully specified in terms of selection/thresholding procedures (e.g., bootstrap ratios, multiple-comparison framework), hampering reproducibility and interpretability.
Potential clinical confounds (e.g., depression/anxiety, medications, sleep apnea) not clearly controlled in the insomnia dataset.
Minor terminology and typographical inconsistencies (e.g., Brainnetome/“Brainconnectome,” punctuation in atlas naming) and occasional grammatical slips that could be polished.
Effect sizes for prediction (r≈0.18) could be contextualized with additional performance metrics (MAE/RMSE) and calibration analyses for clearer practical interpretation.
Limited discussion of dynamic FC literature and its relevance to arousal/attention fluctuations in sleep health.
A more direct comparison with other sleep-disorder biomarkers (including EEG-based graph approaches) would help situate the gains and trade-offs across modalities and datasets.
Potential circularity: day/night periods are inferred from the same neural signal later used to quantify day–night differences, which may bias effect sizes and directionality.
Single narrowband sensing (±2 Hz around a clinician-chosen frequency) introduces selection bias and limits the ability to disentangle periodic peaks from aperiodic 1/f changes across subjects.
No explicit decomposition of periodic versus aperiodic components (e.g., FOOOF), which could confound conclusions about band-limited oscillations versus broadband arousal-related shifts.
Statistical analyses do not account for within-subject correlation between hemispheres; simple ANOVAs and non-parametric tests may overstate precision without mixed-effects modeling.

Absence of sleep staging or independent behavioral/physiological measures (actigraphy, polysomnography) for the full cohort limits the interpretability of “nighttime” effects and sleep-stage specificity.
STN subgroup is relatively small (n=30 hemispheres), reducing power to detect medication/stimulation interactions and limiting cross-target generalizability.

GLM covariates are limited; potential confounders (e.g., disease severity proxies beyond preop UPDRS, medication schedules/agonists, lead localization in motor territory, aperiodic slope) are not included.
The z-score normalization approach (±12 h window) may attenuate or distort circadian amplitudes; its impact on inferred differences is not quantified via sensitivity analyses.

Ambiguity in how z-scoring was computed (sliding vs global), how missing data and outliers were handled, and how significance for “no day–night difference” was determined given the day/night selection procedure.
Limited quantification of effect sizes (e.g., absolute/percent changes in power) and of within-subject reliability (day-to-day stability, cosine fits, or phase consistency).
The rationale for band definitions and grouping of variable center frequencies (especially near 13 Hz) could be better justified and sensitivity-tested.
While key PD sleep/DBS works are cited, there is little engagement with methods for circadian modeling from other domains (e.g., Bayesian circadian HMMs) that could improve state segmentation.
No discussion of separating oscillatory versus aperiodic LFP components despite relevance to arousal/sleep (common in EEG/iEEG literature).
Limited linkage to recent work on learning long-timescale neural dynamics that could inform adaptive control with time-of-day priors.

Rhythm isolation relies on band-pass filtering of band-power time series with limited methodological detail (filter type/order, phase handling, edge-treatment, handling of missing data), raising concerns about leakage and boundary effects, especially for long periods with short recordings.
The coarse binning of ultradian bands (e.g., 1–3 h, 3–6 h, etc.) may conflate episodic fluctuations with true rhythmicity and may not adapt to inter-individual variability in timescales.
Potential confounds include referencing scheme, contact type (depth vs subdural), SNR/impedance differences, distance to white matter, and spectral slope changes; these are only partially addressed.

Sleep/wake and sleep stage are not modeled, though they likely drive much of the circadian and ultradian band-power modulation; this raises interpretability concerns regarding whether diminished rhythms reflect altered local sleep physiology, a central point acknowledged but not probed with proxies.
Medication tapering, post-implant effects, hospital routines, and anesthesia can substantially modulate rhythms; there is no systematic control for these factors across days or subjects.

Heterogeneity in recording duration (2–21 days) impacts reliability of the longest periods; analyses do not clearly weight patients by number of observable cycles or assess per-subject confidence for each cycle band.

Independence of peri-ictal vs inter-ictal effects is assessed with rolling AUC and paired t-tests; given autocorrelation and unequal sample sizes, more robust nonparametric or mixed-effects approaches may be preferable.

Orientation of ROC/AUC labeling (what constitutes the positive class) is not completely transparent; stating explicitly why AUC>0.5 indicates diminished magnitude in pathological tissue would avoid confusion.
Details on artifact rejection, handling of dropouts, and whether seizures and high-IED-rate segments were excluded or modeled are sparse.
The mapping from contacts to ROIs and any within-ROI aggregation steps (median vs mean, weighting) need clearer exposition.

Limited methodological triangulation (e.g., cosinor, Lomb–Scargle periodograms for uneven sampling, wavelet/spectral peak analyses, or EMD/Hilbert-based approaches) to corroborate filter-based findings.

Broader positioning relative to literature on local sleep dynamics (local slow waves, regional sleep homeostasis) and network-level chronobiology in epilepsy could be better integrated to contextualize mechanisms.
